participants.extracted: Pregnant adult women living in a malaria-endemic area in southern Mozambique (Manhiça District) who were enrolled at antenatal care if they were ≤28 weeks’ gestation, permanent residents of the study area, and had no reported sulpha allergy. Of 1,030 randomized women, 515 received sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) plus long-lasting insecticide-treated nets (ITNs) and 515 received placebo plus ITNs; 85% were tested for HIV and 23.6% were HIV-positive.

interventions.extracted: Women were randomized to receive two intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) doses of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP; 1500 mg sulphadoxine/75 mg pyrimethamine, 3 tablets) or identical placebo. The study nurse administered the first dose after at least 12 weeks’ gestation, and the second dose at least one month later, from the second trimester onward. All enrolled women also received a long-lasting insecticide-treated net (LLITN) at recruitment.

outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: low birth weight (LBW), defined as birth weight <2500 g, measured at delivery among live births. Secondary outcomes: prematurity/preterm birth, assessed by gestational age at delivery (gestational age measured at delivery using Dubowitz’s method); maternal anemia, defined by packed cell volume (PCV) <33% for overall anemia and <21% for severe anemia, measured at delivery; maternal malaria parasitemia/P. falciparum parasitemia, defined as asexual parasites of any density on blood smear, measured during pregnancy as first/only clinical malaria episode and at delivery; clinical malaria, defined as parasitemia plus axillary temperature ≥37.5°C, assessed from recruitment through 8 weeks postpartum; placental malaria/infection, classified histologically and by impression smear as any placental infection and as active infection, assessed at delivery; fetal anemia, assessed at birth from cord blood/venous blood; neonatal death/early neonatal death, assessed after delivery; infant outcomes including anemia and clinical malaria/parasitemia, assessed at 8 weeks postpartum; maternal parasitemia and anemia at 8 weeks postpartum, assessed by blood smear and PCV; and safety/tolerability outcomes including hospital admissions, outpatient visits, vomiting, and skin reactions during pregnancy and postpartum through 8 weeks after delivery.
